feedback.text: Student of the Jean Monnet Modules project “DERECHO”: ‘Defense of European Rights and Excellence for Consumers HHarmonisation’, No 101239437. Project financed by the European Union. Jean Monnet Actions in the field of Higher Education: Modules. Call for tenders ERASMUS-JMO-2025-HEI-TCH-RSCH. The autonomy of will and freedom of contract has been restricted for decades, driven by the need for speed in the new contract models and general terms and conditions. In order to mitigate the effects of the inequality between entrepreneurs and consumers and users arising from this situation, the European Union and its Member States have developed legislation on the protection of the former. Today, with the development of a culture of proximity in digital environments, the need for speed is further boosted and consumer protection should therefore also be increased by restrictions on consumer rights due to reduced free will and consent. In the face of this situation, the European Union must take action not only to cover its task of protecting citizens but also to regularise and harmonise the provisions of all its members. The Digital Fairness Act can achieve this, but to this end, according to the vision of a student of law and international relations at CEU San Pablo in Madrid, Spain and part of the Jean Monnet project, there are certain provisions that it should contain. Firstly, property rights on tangible things over intangible assets in the digital environment should be equated. Not only because the property rights obtained are in the context of a computer, mobile phone or any other electronic device means that they have less value than those received directly from a business premises or those which, although telematically contracted, are sent to the consumer’s home. The protection of these rights must be reflected in the essential concepts of civil law of enjoyment, enjoyment and disposition. These minimum rights should be guaranteed and in addition to this, withdrawal, guarantees, relevant actions, etc. In this context, I would like to comment on the case of the “stop Destroying videogames” initiative, a project that is a driving force for the defence of digital property and which has a strong place in the Digital Fairness Act. Beyond and as a second essential concept, the new reality of the average consumer targeted by this initiative must be understood. This person, while being circumspect and reasonably informed, the digital reality and the volatile and precipitated world, means that he or she has no time or at least to prove it. We cannot require this average consumer to read membership contracts for each digital service you contract, nor can we expect the conditions for the use of your personal rights to be read. This subtle change in definition becomes important when we realise that this lack of attention can be exploited by entrepreneurs with the so-called dark patterns, addictive design, unfair personalisation practices and other concepts that affect our daily presence in the digital environment. It is therefore impossible to legislate without taking this surrounding reality into account, without knowing the context in which the average consumer develops. As a worried student and as a citizen of the European Union, I am looking forward to this project, with which we can achieve sufficient and comprehensive protection for consumers and users, reducing inequality with entrepreneurs and creating a much safer digital environment for all.
